terms.definition: The order in which statements run, governed by conditionals, loops and jumps such as break and return.
An if/elif/else construct that runs a branch of code only when its condition is truthy.
A construct that repeats a block of code. Python has for (over an iterable) and while (until a condition is false).
Any object you can loop over with for, such as a list, string, dict, range, file or generator.
A statement that immediately exits the nearest enclosing loop.
A statement that skips the rest of the current iteration and moves to the next one.
An else attached to a for/while loop that runs only if the loop completed without executing a break.
An early return or raise at the top of a function that handles invalid or edge cases first, keeping the main logic unnested.
The match/case statement (Python 3.10+, PEP 634) that selects a branch by matching a value against patterns, with case _ as the wildcard.
A no-op placeholder that satisfies Python's need for a statement where one is syntactically required but nothing should happen.
